headline: Brazil's Humpback Whales Stage Remarkable Recovery
meta_description: Humpback whale populations in Brazil have surged 27-fold since 1986, transforming whale watching into a booming tourism industry.
summary_short: From 2,000 to 35,000: Brazil's humpback whales have made an astonishing comeback, transforming coastal communities and inspiring global conservation.
body:
What began as a conservation success story has quietly transformed into something remarkable: a thriving whale-watching economy that's reshaping how Brazilians connect with their ocean.

In Guanabara Bay near Rio de Janeiro, humpback whale populations have surged from just 2,000 in 1986 to approximately 35,000 today. Between June and November each year, these gentle giants migrate from Antarctic feeding grounds toward Abrolhos Bank, a biodiverse coral reef region spanning Bahia and Espirito Santo states—and straight into the cameras of tourists and researchers.

"It's wonderful," says Enrico Marcovaldi, co-founder of the Humpback Whale Project. "It shows that the whales are making a recovery, are healthy and thriving."

Local tourism agencies have embraced this boom. André Bento, president of Niteroi's tourism agency, sees something deeper than economic opportunity. "Tourism can be a tool for the protection of species," he told reporters during a June whale-watching outing. "I don't think anyone who gets on this boat comes off the same way."

Brazil isn't alone. Humpback populations are rebounding globally—from South Africa to eastern Australia to the Salish Sea near Seattle, where hundreds have returned after being absent for years. These sightings remind us that recovery is possible when communities commit to protecting the natural world.
